concept_name: Why Pressure Is a Scalar Even Though It 'Points Everywhere'
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 5.1 — trap quantity; split from scalar_vs_vector bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A pressure cooker. Steam inside pushes outward on every wall — top, bottom, sides, lid — with the same pressure. The cooker has ONE pressure value written on the gauge, not six different vector arrows.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Diving into a swimming pool. At a given depth your ears feel the same squeeze from above, below, left and right. Pressure at a point has one number — the depth determines it, not a direction.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A bicycle tyre inflated to 40 psi. That single number acts outward on every square inch of the inner tube, no matter which way that square inch faces. Pressure is a property of the point, not a pointer in one direction.

formulas.hydrostatic_pressure: P = rho * g * h  (scalar, one number per depth)
formulas.force_from_pressure: F_on_face = P * A * n_hat  (vector — direction set by the face normal n_hat)

constraints: At a point inside a fluid, the pressure is the same in all directions — Pascal's principle
Force is a vector; pressure is the scalar that GENERATES force when combined with area and a surface normal
Pressure is a scalar FIELD — one value at each point in space, like temperature or density

STATE_1.title: At One Point Inside a Fluid — Arrows in Every Direction

scene_composition.text:
Pressure pushes outward
in every direction from point P,
all with the same magnitude.

So which ONE direction
do we assign to the vector?

(none — that's the point.)

tts_sentences.text_en: Zoom in on one tiny point inside a fluid. Say a point five metres below the surface of a swimming pool.
The fluid pushes outward from that point in every direction — up, down, left, right, and every direction in between. All with the same magnitude.
To call something a vector, we must be able to assign it a single direction. But here every direction has the same push. There is no preferred direction to pick.
If you cannot name one direction for the quantity, it cannot be a vector. That is the first clue pressure is a scalar.
Pressure behaves like temperature. Temperature at a point is one number. Pressure at a point is one number too.

STATE_2.title: Pressure Is a Scalar. Force on a Face Is a Vector.

scene_composition.text:
Cube of water submerged at depth h
each face has area A

P at depth h
= rho * g * h
= ONE NUMBER  (scalar)

Force on each face
= P * A * n_hat
  scalar | area | face normal

Pressure supplies magnitude.
The face normal supplies direction.
=> F is a vector, P is a scalar.

tts_sentences.text_en: Drop a small cube of water at depth h, each face with area A. The fluid around it presses on every face.
All four faces feel different force directions — the top face is pushed downward, the bottom face upward, the left face rightward, the right face leftward.
But look at the magnitudes. Every face feels the same magnitude of force — P times A.
Pressure P is the single scalar behind all of them. It equals rho g h at depth h — one number, no direction.
Force is pressure times area times the face normal. Pressure supplies the magnitude. The face normal supplies the direction.
That is the cleanest way to remember it. Force is a vector. Pressure is the scalar that generates forces when combined with a surface orientation.

STATE_3.title: Pressure and Temperature — Same Kind of Thing

scene_composition.text:
Temperature field T(x, y, z)

At any point, one number.
E.g. T = 305 K.

No direction.
Clearly scalar.

Pressure field P(x, y, z)

At any point, one number.
E.g. P = 2 x 10^5 Pa at depth 10 m.

No direction.
Also scalar.

Contrast: gravitational field g(x, y, z)
At each point a magnitude AND a direction
(down). That is a vector field.

tts_sentences.text_en: A clean way to remember this — compare pressure with temperature.
Temperature at a point in the room is one number, say three hundred and five kelvin. No direction. Clearly a scalar.
Pressure at a point in a fluid is one number, say two atmospheres at ten metres deep. No direction. Also a scalar.
Both are scalar fields. One number at every point in space, changing smoothly from point to point.
Compare that with the gravitational field. At every point it has both a magnitude and a direction — pointing downward. That is a vector field.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Pressure is a vector because it 'acts in all directions'
trigger_phrases: pressure acts in all directions so it is a vector

STATE_1.title: Your Belief — Pressure Acts Everywhere, So Vector

scene_composition.text:
Your reasoning

Pressure pushes in all directions.
That looks like many arrows.
Vectors are arrows.
=> pressure is a vector (maybe even many).

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is your intuition. Pressure pushes outward from a point in every direction.
Every direction has an arrow. Arrows mean vectors. So pressure must be a vector.
The problem is precisely that there are too many arrows, not too few. Vectors have ONE direction — not many. Let us see why that matters.

STATE_2.title: The Direction Problem — There Is No 'One' Direction

scene_composition.text:
Pressure at the same point

Arrow up    ? yes
Arrow down  ? yes
Arrow east  ? yes
Arrow west  ? yes

If I ask you 'which ONE direction?'
you cannot answer.
=> not a vector.

tts_sentences.text_en: Force on a ball has one direction — downward, say. Velocity of a car has one direction — eastward.
To assign a vector, we must pick exactly one direction at each point. That is what 'has a direction' really means.
At a point inside a fluid, pressure goes in every direction with equal magnitude. If you ask 'which one direction?' there is no answer.
Equal strength everywhere is the same as no preferred direction. And no preferred direction means no vector.

STATE_3.title: Force Is the Vector. Pressure Is the Scalar Behind It.

scene_composition.text:
F = P * A * n_hat

F   vector (force on the surface)
P   scalar (pressure, one number)
A   scalar (area, one number)
n_hat  unit vector (surface normal)

The direction of F comes from n_hat,
not from P.

Same point. Three faces.

Top face: F = PA down
Side face: F = PA horizontal
Bottom face: F = PA up

P is the same number in all three.
The direction changes because the
face orientation changes — not because
pressure has a direction.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the relationship cleanly. Force equals pressure times area times the face normal.
Force is a vector. Pressure is a scalar. Area is a scalar. The face normal, n-hat, is a unit vector.
The direction of the force comes entirely from n-hat — the way the surface faces. Not from the pressure itself.
Pressure at a point has one number. Different surfaces passing through that point pick out different directions, but the pressure is unchanged.
So when someone says pressure has a direction, what they mean is that pressure generates forces, and forces have direction. Pressure itself does not.

STATE_4.title: Quick Check — You Can Now Classify These

scene_composition.text:
Apply the new rule

Temperature in a room:
  one number per point, no direction
  => SCALAR

Gravitational field:
  at each point, a magnitude AND
  a direction (downward)
  => VECTOR

Pressure in a pressure cooker:
  one number per point, no direction
  => SCALAR

Electric field of a charge:
  at each point, magnitude AND
  radial direction
  => VECTOR

tts_sentences.text_en: Apply the rule. For each quantity, ask: at one point in space, does it have a magnitude and exactly one direction?
Temperature — one number, no direction — scalar.
Gravitational field — magnitude g, direction straight down — vector.
Pressure in a cooker — one number at each point, no single direction — scalar.
Electric field of a charge — magnitude decreases with distance, direction radially outward — vector.
That rule will carry you through every assertion-reason trap in this section.

regeneration_variants.label: Different fluids, same rule
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Water (rho = 1000), mercury (rho = 13600), seawater (rho = 1025) at various depths — pressure is rho g h every time, still scalar.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: P is a scalar field whose value scales linearly with depth

regeneration_variants.label: Gas pressure inside a closed vessel
regeneration_variants.physical_world: A sealed pressure cooker at 2 atm has uniform pressure throughout. Walls feel force = P * A perpendicular to each wall — different directions, same scalar P.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Pressure is location-dependent only; force direction comes from surface orientation
